coredata.dc:title: Highly stable multicolor carbon dots created using o-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid systems towards flexible emitting films
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Yi
Yue, Yunlong
coredata.dc:description: Multicolor carbon dots (CDs) with high stability are desired for various light emitting devices and bio-images. In this paper, the green and red emitting CDs were created by o-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid (TA) as precursors at different temperatures via a solvothermal reaction. An efficient response surface methodology is developed to analyze the formation mechanism. Using optimized conditions, the green and red emitting CDs reveal photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of 34.0 % and 26.8 %, respectively. The result of response surface method indicates that temperature, precursor concentration, and TA amount significantly influence the PLQYs of the CDs. The formation mechanism of the CDs was proposed by analyzing solvatochromism phenomena, oxidation processes, and fluorescence lifetimes. Initially, green CDs with surface state luminescence were formed followed by the subsequent generation of red CDs exhibiting molecular and carbon core state emission during oxidation. These CDs reveal high stability, in which the PL intensity almost remained unchanged after 7 months. Furthermore, three-color CDs/polymer films (λem = 500, 536, and 568 nm) were successfully fabricated by using these two types of CDs. Sample 180-CDs/polystyrene film had photochromic properties, while other films had good photostability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
O-phenylenediamine
Film
Multi-color emitting

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported partly by the National Natural Science Foundation of China , China (grant no. 51972145 ), Ji Nan Science & Technology Bureau , China (No. 2021GXRC109 ), and Science and Technology Program of University of Jinan , China ( XKY2118 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–d) TEM images of sample 180-CDs. (e-f) TEM images of sample 220-CDs.

(a–d) XPS spectra of samples 180-CDs and 220-CDs: (a) XPS survey spectra, (b) O1s spectra, (c) C1s spectra, (d) N1s spectra. (e) XRD patterns of sample 180-CDs and 220-CDs. (f) FT-IR spectra of sample 180-CDs and 220-CDs.

(a) Absorption and PL emission spectra (λex = 450 nm) of sample 180-CDs. (b) Absorption and PL emission spectra (λex =535 nm) of sample 220-CDs. (c) PL emission spectra of sample 180-CDs in different concentrations (bottom to top for concentrations from 0.0063 to 2.5000 mg/mL, the increments between concentrations were consistent). (d) PL emission spectra of sample 220-CDs dispersed in different concentrations (bottom to top for concentrations from 0.0063 to 2.5000 mg/mL). (e) PL emission spectra of sample 180-CDs dispersed in aprotic solvents. (f) PL emission spectra of sample 180-CDs dispersed in protic solvents. Insets in (a) and (b) show the photographs of the CDs under room light and 365 nm UV light radiation.
Three-dimensional (3D) response surface for interaction effect of g-CDs.
3D response surface for interaction effect of r-CDs.

(a) PL spectra of sample A-CDs and A-H2O2-CDs. (b) PL spectra of sample B-CDs and B-H2O2-CDs. (c) FT-IR spectrum of sample 180-CDs, 220-CDs and B-H2O2-CDs. (d) Fluorescence decay curves of sample 180-CDs, 220-CDs and B-H2O2-CDs, λex = 370 nm. (e) schematic diagram of formation mechanism of CDs. (A: 12.5 g/L, oPD/(oPD+TA) = 0.20, 180 °C, 8 h; B: 300.0 g/L, oPD/(oPD+TA) = 0.66, 220 °C, 12 h).

(a) Pictures of sample 180-CDs in PS film, 220-CDs in PS films, sample 220-CDs in PVA films, and sample 180-CDs in PVA films exposed to 365 nm UV light for 9 h. (b) PL spectra of sample 180-CDs and 180-CDs before and after storing for 210 days. (c) PL spectra of samples 220-CDs and 220-CDs before and after storing for 210 days. (d) PL spectra of sample 180-CDs in PS film exposed to 365 nm UV light. (e) PL spectra of sample 220-CDs in PS film exposed to 365 nm UV light. (f) Pictures of CDs in films under 365 nm UV light. (g) PL spectra of sample 180-CDs in PVA film exposed to 365 nm UV light. (h) PL spectra of sample 220-CDs in PVA film exposed to 365 nm UV light.
PL properties and average lifetime τavg, time constants τ1, τ2 and τ3, components B1, B2 and B3 of samples.

head.ce:title: Highly stable multicolor carbon dots created using o-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid systems towards flexible emitting films

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multicolor carbon dots (CDs) with high stability are desired for various light emitting devices and bio-images. In this paper, the green and red emitting CDs were created by o-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid (TA) as precursors at different temperatures via a solvothermal reaction. An efficient response surface methodology is developed to analyze the formation mechanism. Using optimized conditions, the green and red emitting CDs reveal photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of 34.0 % and 26.8 %, respectively. The result of response surface method indicates that temperature, precursor concentration, and TA amount significantly influence the PLQYs of the CDs. The formation mechanism of the CDs was proposed by analyzing solvatochromism phenomena, oxidation processes, and fluorescence lifetimes. Initially, green CDs with surface state luminescence were formed followed by the subsequent generation of red CDs exhibiting molecular and carbon core state emission during oxidation. These CDs reveal high stability, in which the PL intensity almost remained unchanged after 7 months. Furthermore, three-color CDs/polymer films (λem = 500, 536, and 568 nm) were successfully fabricated by using these two types of CDs. Sample 180-CDs/polystyrene film had photochromic properties, while other films had good photostability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging nano-fluorescent material with rich physicochemical and optical properties, good biocompatibility, high stability, and low cost, carbon dots (CDs) have become a hot topic in the fields of energy conversion [1], photoelectric chemistry [2] and biomedicine [3]. In particular, the red CDs have important application value in the fields of white light LED, and biological imaging [4,5]. However, most CDs fluoresce blue, and the preparation of red CDs is still difficult [6]. Efficient design and controlled synthesis of green to red-emitting CDs with high stability is more crucial in restricting the application of CDs [6–8]. In terms of synthesis methods, compared with top-down methods, bottom-up methods have low equipment requirements and mild reaction conditions and are therefore currently more widely adopted [9]. Solvothermal synthesis, as one of the bottom-up methods, has advantages because of simple preparation and controllable carbonization degree compared with microwave and ultrasonic methods. Thus, it has become the mainstream method for CDs synthesis [10]. However, the reaction parameters of the solvothermal synthesis have complex effects on the physicochemical properties of CDs, which means it is a challenge to systematically study the effect of each reaction parameter on the fluorescence properties of the CDs. Currently, machine learning is commonly used to predict the luminescence of CDs, but it requires a large number of experimental samples with a tedious workload [11,12]. In the actual synthesis process, the mainstream research approach is still the trial-and-error method with controlled reaction variables [13]. Therefore, efficient response surface methodology can be used to study the effect of each parameter on the photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of CDs, but such studies are still in their infancy with a few reports [14,15].

Small aromatic ring molecules are commonly used as starting materials for the preparation of CDs by a solvothermal method, which have excellent PL properties due to the conjugated structure, and the emission wavelength covers the range from blue to near infrared [16]. At present, there have been abundant studies on the preparation of CDs by solvothermal synthesis using phenylenediamine as precursors because phenylenediamine has an aromatic ring as a conjugate structure and nitrogen element as a heteroatom. In 2015, Jiang et al. prepared CDs with blue, green, and red emitting for the first time using three tautomeric systems of phenylenediamine. They concluded that the differences in particle size and nitrogen content led to different luminescence colors [17]. In 2018, Liu et al. prepared red-emitting CDs from o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and found that H+ and NO3 - must act together to prepare red-emitting CDs, and the size of the conjugated aromatic π system of CDs can be adjusted by adjusting the amount of HNO3 [18]. Wang et al. prepared multicolor CDs by mixing oPD with different acidic reagents as precursors [19]. Ji and co-workers synthesized kilograms of near-infrared CDs from oPD, 3-chloroperbenzoic acid, and H2SO4 aqueous solution in a household microwave oven [20]. However, in the preparation process of such CDs, inorganic acids, which are highly corrosive to the equipment, are usually used. Moreover, the CDs is usually purified by dialysis and column chromatography [21–23]. These shortcomings limit the industrial production of CDs. Therefore, it is a prospective research direction to explore how to synthesize CDs by a simple method without inorganic acids.

Although the research on oPD-based CDs is deepening, there are still problems such as low quantum yields and unclear luminescence mechanisms. Fewer studies have been reported on the color shift process or tuning method of CDs in the blue to red wavelength range [6]. The explanations that determine the fluorescence of oPD-based CDs with different colors are diverse, including quantum-limited effects [19], carbon core states, surface functional groups [4], molecular state luminescence [24], etc. The mechanism of formation of CDs synthesized from oPD is still controversial. Most researchers believe that the two molecules of oPD will firstly self-condense to form 2,3-phennazinediamine (DAP), and the presence of oxidizing agents or acids can increase the degree of polymerization, which in turn promotes this process [20,25]. Zhang et al. suggested that the protonation and deprotonation processes of DAP change the state of the molecular fluorophore and thus the photon leap band gap, leading to the conversion of yellow fluorescence and red fluorescence [24]. Wang et al. prepared six different oPD-derived CDs and found that they had similar carbon core structures but different surface polymer shells. They proposed that the carbon core regulates emission wavelength, and the polymer shell regulates the emission intensity [26]. A unified understanding has not yet been developed and further studies are needed.

In this work, green and red emitting CDs with high PLQYs and stability were prepared by solvothermal synthesis using oPD and terephthalic acid (TA) without inorganic acids or subsequent purification processes. The luminescent properties of the CDs were investigated in terms of both reaction parameters and formation pathways. The effect of reaction conditions on the PLQYs of CDs was investigated using the response surface methodology. Hydrogen peroxide was added to the reaction system to analyze the change in multicolor CDs composition. Three color-emitting films were prepared by combining CDs with polymer and the stability of CDs in solution and in polymer films was investigated. This work provides a reference for understanding the formation process and PL properties of multicolor CDs.

oPD, TA, ethanol (99.5 %), and hydrogen peroxide (30 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Polystyrene (PS, General Purpose Type I), Rhodamine 6G, 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAPN), quinoxalino [2,3-b] phenazine-2,3-diamine (QXPDA) and 5,14-dihydroquinoxalino[2,3-b] phenazine (DHQP) were provided by McLean. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, MW = 44.05) was purchased from Aladdin. All chemicals were obtained from commercial suppliers and used without further purification.

In a typical CDs preparation process, 0.083 g of oPD and 0.042 g of TA were added in 10 mL ethanol with ultrasonication for 10 min, then transferred into a 30 mL reaction kettle with Teflon lining, heated in the oven to the specified temperature for the specified time, and cooled to room temperature naturally after the reaction. The CDs prepared at 180 °C was labeled as 180-CDs and the CDs prepared at 220 °C was labeled as 220-CDs. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was filtered by 0.22 µm organic filter membrane and stored at room temperature·H2O2-CDs were prepared by adding 10 μL of 30 % hydrogen peroxide solution to ethanol before the reaction started. Other reaction conditions were consistent with ordinary CDs. The solvothermal reaction was carried out in a constant temperature oven. The sample 220-CDs were separated by column chromatography using a mixture of petroleum ether and ethyl acetate (3/1, v/v). The green and red CDs were isolated and named g-CDs and r-CDs.

The morphologies of samples were observed by Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (JEOL 2100, Japan). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was collected by D8-ADVANCE (Bruker Corporation, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (USA). Sample were irradiated with a monochromatic Al K (1486.6 eV) radiation (12 kV; 6 mA). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on VG Micro Tech ESCA 300 (Thermo Electron, the USA) infrared spectrophotometer using the KBr tablet technique. The UV–visible (absorption) spectra were measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-4100, Japan). The PL spectra were recorded from a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4600, Japan). The fluorescence decay plots were obtained from Jobin Yvon IBH fluorescence lifetime analyzer (Horiba, Japan).

For CDs/PS film preparation, 0.4 g of PS was dissolved in 4 mL toluene with 0.25 mL of CDs solution (≈ 3.125 mg) with magnetic stirring under heating for 10 min. A transparent solution was applied on the petri dish and dry naturally for 1 day to obtain the CDs/PS film, which was then cut to the appropriate size. The PVA film was prepared in a similar manner to the PS film, with the PS replaced by PVA, and the toluene replaced by water.

For stability test, the CDs solution was stored at room temperature and protected from light for 7 months to test its long-term storage stability. The CDs/polymer films were conducted under the irradiation of a portable handheld ultraviolet (UV) lamp of 365 nm. The films were continuously irradiated by placing them in 5 cm from the UV lamp in a dark environment, and their PL intensity was recorded at intervals.

The PLQYs of CDs was determined according to Eq. (1).
(1) Φₓ=Φst[Gradₓ/Gradst]*[ηₓ²/η²st]
Where, Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, grad denotes the slope of the integrated area of the emission peak with respect to the absorbance. The subscripts X and ST denote the CDs and reference sample Rhodamine 6G with a PLQY of 98 % in ethanol, respectively. η denotes the refractive index of the solvent.

As shown in Fig. 1a–d, the size of sample 180-CDs was uniform, and the CDs revealed well crystallization. Two kinds of lattice fringes of 0.21 nm and 0.29 nm observed, corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes of graphene, respectively. The average size of 180-CDs was 7.05 nm while the 220-CDs exhibited an average size of 1.35 nm (Fig. S1a and S1b, see Supporting Information). The red emitting CDs may come from the polymerization of small molecules or the pyrolysis of larger CDs [10]. Although lattice fringes observed in some CDs, the lattice fringes of CDs in another area were not clearly, suggesting that the CDs may form a certain accumulation structure. This indicates that the aggregation of CDs with polymers or molecular clumps may change the structure of the carbon dots and thus affect the luminescence properties, resulting in a blue shift of the emission wavelength, which is discussed later in this work [27].

The XPS survey spectra in Fig. 2a and the components in Table S1 (see Supporting Information) indicate that increasing temperature caused the N component of the CDs to decrease, which was consistent with the speculation that the oxidation of DAPN fluorophore forms red fluorescence. Element N may spill out of the reaction system in the form of NOx as oxynitride, thus reducing the amino functional group attached to the benzene ring and leading to a shortening of the conjugated π-bond and resulting inhigher emission energy [28]. Increasing the oxygen component for sample 220-CDs indicates that the surface of the sample was rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. The O1s spectra and C1s spectra in Figs. 2b and 2c show that both samples 180-CDs and 220-CDs have abundant conjugated structures, amides, carboxyl groups, and other functional groups. In terms of element N in Fig. 2d, sample 180-CDs revealed pyridine nitrogen (398.6 eV), amide nitrogen (399.4 eV), and pyrrole nitrogen (400.9 eV) [4,29]. No peak of pyridine nitrogen at the red carbon site, and both pyrrole N and amide N increased, which is consistent with the result reported in the literature [30]. When the amount of amide on the surface increased, the sp2 conjugation between the lone pair electrons and carbon core became much more significant, resulting in the PL spectrum redshifted and the band gap decreased [4].

The XRD patterns of samples 180-CDs and 220-CDs were similar as shown in Fig. 2e, with a wide peak at about 24.5°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, and a peak at about 43.0°, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. The XRD peak of sample 220-CDs related (100) plane was obvious, and the shift to the right was 1.7°, indicating a higher degree of graphitization and a smaller spacing between crystal facets. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the XRD peak at 27.8° for sample 180-CDs was smaller than that of sample 220-CDs, indicating that the particle size of 180-CDs was large, which is consistent with the results of TEM observation.

The infrared spectra of sample 180-CDs and sample 220-CDs, as depicted in Fig. 2f, exhibited significant deviations from the precursor opd or TA within the fingerprint region. Moreover, they were not a mere superposition of the two spectra, indicating the absence of unreacted precursors within the CDs. The functional groups of sample 180-CDs were abundant, corresponding to N-H bond, O-H bond, and = C-H bond at 3550, 3415, and 3030 cm−1 respectively. The dense peaks near 3360 cm−1 indicated abundant amine bond and amide bond existed [31]. The peaks at 3238 and 3288 cm−1 were attributed to N-H stretching and C-N-H bending, respectively. The peaks corresponding to CO (1639 cm−1), CN (1614 cm−1), N-H (1502 cm−1), C-N (1398 cm−1), C-O-C (1051 cm−1) and the stretching vibration of phenazine structure at 748 cm−1 suggest the abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of 180-CDs. The peak intensity of sample 220-CDs was significantly lower compared with that of sample 180-CDs. The aromatic ring peak at 1600–1450 cm−1 confirmed sample 220-CDs with a conjugated structure, and the symmetric stretching of C-O-H outward bending of the carboxylic acid or the C-O-C of carboxylic anhydride at 951 cm−1 were enhanced. The enhanced O-H bond proved that the sample 220-CDs contained abundant hydrogen bonds, which enabled the molecular spacing to be less than 10 nm, thus enabling the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process [32]. The peaks of CO and CN at 1639 and 1614 cm−1 suggest that sample 220-CDs contained many oxygen-containing functional groups which is consistent with XPS analysis.

To investigate the effect of temperature on the PL properties of CDs, the absorption, photoexcitation, and PL spectra of samples 180-CDs and 220-CDs were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 3a and Fig. S2a (see Supporting Information), the optimized PL excitation wavelength of sample 180-CDs was 450 nm while the optimized PL emission wavelength was 540 nm.

which is related to a standard green light with a FWHM of PL spectrum of 83 nm and good monochromaticity. As shown in Fig. 3b and Fig. S2b (see Supporting Information), the excitation and PL peak wavelengths of sample 220-CDs were 535 and 600 nm, respectively. The PL peak was accompanied by a shoulder peak at 650 nm, which was a common emission peak from oPD-based CDs [26,33]. The PLQYs of 180-CDs and 220-CDs were 34.0 % and 26.8 %, respectively. Comparing the absorption spectra, both samples 180-CDs and 220-CDs revealed absorption peaks at around 240 and 280 nm, which corresponded to CC/ CN and the n-π * leap of CO, respectively. The difference is that 180-CDs have absorption peaks at 430 nm, corresponding to the luminescence from the surface state while 220-CDs have absorption peaks at 546 nm and 578 nm. This is ascribed to the different molecular states and conjugated structures [4,27]. In addition, the absorption peak of sample 220-CDs was in the excitation spectral region of sample 180-CDs, therefore sample 180-CDs can act as an energy donor and sample 220-CDs as an energy acceptor to realize the FRET. The FRET process ultimately contributes to the equilibration of PL intensity in multicolor CDs, which can be verified by examining the normalized PL spectra of CDs at various concentrations [4].

Fig. 3c and d show the PL spectra of samples 180-CDs and 220-CDs at various concentrations. Fig. S3a and S3b (see Supporting Information) show the pictures of sample 180-CDs at different concentrations. Fig. 3c shows that at low concentration conditions, sample 180-CDs revealed two peaks at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, one in the short wavelength region of 400–500 nm and the other in the long wavelength region of about 540 nm. As concentration increased, the peak at the short wavelength gradually disappeared and with a redshift of 8 nm in the long wavelength region of PL spectrum (543–550 nm). This is due to the large crystal plane spacing of green CDs, eliminating dipole-dipole and π-π stacking interactions between adjacent optical chromophores, thereby suppressing intermolecular charge transfer [31].

Fig. 3d shows that at low concentrations, sample 220-CDs revealed blue emitting, which is due to as-prepared red CDs generally containing a slight amount of blue emitting [25,27]. It is worth noting that precursor TA can react with hydrogen peroxide to produce a fluorescent substance 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid, whose emission wavelength is at 425 nm, which is close to the position of the blue fluorescence peak. To investigate whether the blue emitting component was an impurity, sample 220-CDs was purified by column chromatography. Surprisingly, there was no isolated blue component in sample 220-CDs. PL spectra of isolated red CDs at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. S4 (see Supporting Information). At the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the r-CDs emitted blue luminescence with high intensity, indicating that the blue luminous component was not a separable impurity but rather exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent behavior. The associated peak of the red spectrum at 650 nm after column chromatography disappeared, indicating that the red luminous component is not unique, and the luminous peak at 650 nm may come from the interaction between the multi-molecules in the system. The absorption spectra and PL emission spectra of g-CDs shown in Fig. S5a and S5b (see Supporting Information) were basically consistent with those of sample 180-CDs. The r-CDs exhibited blue emission (λex = 365 nm, λem = 432 nm), which essentially matched the excitation spectrum of g-CDs. It indicated that r-CDs could serve as the energy donor of g-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and the blue-fluorescence can be absorbed by g-CDs and converted into the emitted light at 540 nm. Fig. S5b (see Supporting Information) displayed the absorption spectra and emission spectra of g-CDs at 500–600 nm of normalized sample 220-CDs. The significant overlap spectra suggested that g-CDs could function as an energy donor, while the red component could act as an energy receptor. The light emitted by the g-CDs at 540 nm can be absorbed by r-CDs and converted into red-fluorescence at 600 nm. This demonstrated that there was potential for FRET process between r-CDs and g-CDs.

As concentration increased, the intensity of the PL peak at 600 nm was significantly enhanced compared with the PL peaks at 410–450 nm. This is also visible in the PL spectra obtained using different excitation wavelengths in the excitation spectra (Figs. S3j-3l, see Supporting Information), indicating that increased concentration decreased the spacing between the donor and acceptor molecules to resonant energy transfer. Blue emitting was absorbed by acceptor molecules, confirming the FRET process [4]. As shown in Figs. S3g-3i (see Supporting Information), the PL shows a transition process from blue to red with concentration increased. At low concentrations, blue emitting CDs were more pronounced while the red PL dominated at high concentrations.

To further verify the role of surface states in PL, the CDs were dispersed in different solvents. Figs. 3e and 3f show the PL emission spectra of sample 180-CDs dispersed in aprotic and protonic solvents at concentrations of 0.0625 mg/mL respectively. Figs. S3e-3f show that the emitting color of sample 180-CDs changed from green to orange. From Fig. 3e and Table S2 (see Supporting Information), the PL peak wavelengths of sample 180-CDs kept red-shift with increasing solvent polarity, which came from the dipole-dipole interaction between CDs and solvent molecules as well as the interaction of surface functional groups with hydrogen bonds in the solvent [34]. The redshift was more pronounced in the aprotic solvent, suggesting that the dipole moment has a large influence on the electronic structure of the surface. It is suggested that the abundant nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of sample 180-CDsd increased carrier density and promoted the electron transfer from the sp2 carbon nucleus to the edge [29]. In protonic solvents (Fig. 3f), the CDs in ethanol revealed a similar PL peak with small redshifts even though ethanol with different polarities, indicating that the nitrogen/oxygen atoms on the surface of the CDs with powerful interactions with the alcohol hydroxyl groups. Thus, the excited states are stable [35,36]. This demonstrates a variation in time changing the structure and composition of CDs, causing the emitting from green to red. However, it remains to be investigated whether reaction parameters such as concentration, fraction of raw material, and time affect the fluorescence properties of CDs.

Reaction conditions play important roles during CDs synthesis. The concentration and ratios of precursors directly affect the surface functional groups of CDs. The temperature affected the formation rate of CDs and the degree of graphitization [10]. Fig. S6 (see Supporting Information) shows the fluorescence spectra of CDs at different synthesis temperatures. Fig. S6a (see Supporting Information) demonstrated that the sample 180-CDs exhibited typical green fluorescence emission and excitation wavelength independence, indicating the presence of uniform surface states in most CDs [37]. A minor amount of red luminescent components was generated at longer wavelengths which also displayed excitation wavelength independence, suggesting the coexistence of green and red luminescent centers. As the synthesis temperature increased, the fluorescence intensity of green emission gradually decreased while the intensity of red emission increased, implying a possible transition from green to red carbon dots. The optimal emission peak experienced a redshift from 540 nm to 600 nm without an intermediate state, indicating that this change originated from alterations in fluorophore molecular structure rather than concentration-induced aggregation effects or surface passivation [38]. At low excitation wavelengths, high-temperature synthesized CDs emitted blue light with excitation wavelength dependence, demonstrating that the 220-CDs possessed both the excitation wavelength-dependent and independent PL emissions [39].

To investigate systematically the effect of parameters on the PL properties, the effect of parameters on PLQYs was investigated by a response surface method. Response surface methodology has become a powerful tool for studying CDs, avoiding the disadvantages of complex single-factor tests that do not reflect the interactions of parameters, allowing a reduction in the amount of trial and error, and assessing the effects of specific parameters and their possible interaction response [14,15,40]. Here, the effect of four parameters on the PLQYs of green emitting CDs (g-CDs, λem=540 nm) and red emitting CDs (r-CDs, λem=600 nm) were investigated. The experimental data are shown in Table S8-S7 (see Supporting Information). The variable values and coding levels are presented in Tables S8. A block was built in this experiment and the midpoint experiment was repeated 5 times.

The Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) in Table S7 (see Supporting Information) illustrates that the p-value of this model is less than 0.0001, indicating that this model is greatly significant for the PLQYs of green CDs. The ratio of oPD and TA had a small effect on the PLQYs, but it was in accordance with the basic law of the volcano curve, which implied a high PLQYs for oPD/(oPD+TA) of 0.6 ( Fig. 4a, c, f), which indicated that TA and oPD were co-involved in the formation of CDs. On the other hand, temperature and concentration were critical factors for the PLQYs of g-CDs. The interactions between temperature and concentration, as well as temperature and time, considerably affected the fluorescence intensity of CDs (p < 0.0001). Interestingly, Fig. 4 shows that the plots of the g-CDs are basin-shaped, which is different from the common peak-shaped 3D response surface plot, indicating the complexity of the luminescence properties of the g-CDs.

The green emitting component revealed high PLQYs at low temperature, short time, and lower concentration, relating to a low activation energy. However, if the temperature continued to rise or the reaction time increased, the surface functional groups of the CDs would be over-modified, resulting in the reduction of defects, the disappearance of luminescent groups, or excessive carbonization of carbon core, which in turn leads to the weakening of the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4b–d). Further increasing the temperature/concentration or reaction time, the PLQYs pick up, supposedly forming new luminous centers on the surface of the CDs.

For red emitting, too high temperature, concentration, or prolonged time likewise reduce the PLQYs since the fluorescent center may be destroyed. In addition, the ratio of TA to oPD had a significant effect on the PLQYs. In the range of oPD/(oPD+TA) of 0.2–1, the lower the content of oPD, the high PLQYs ( Fig. 5a, d, e), indicating TA played an important role in red-emitting CDs.

Firstly, its rigid structure facilitated the formation of sp2 planar structure and increased conjugated π-bonds, which induced intramolecular charge transfer. Secondly, the carboxyl group on TA was electronegative and could act as an electron acceptor to combine with the amino group of the strong electron-giving group on oPD to form a donor-π-acceptor structure to modify and passivate the CDs surface [30].

By designing experiments and modeling through response surface methodology, the effect of reaction parameters on the PLQY for green and red emitting CDs was found. Multi-color emitting CDs were prepared by adjusting the reaction parameters. The conditions and properties are shown in Table S8 and Fig. S7 (see Supporting Information). However, the formation process of the CDs is unclear. In addition, it is surprising that the blue emitting component (λem=406–410 nm) was produced under some reaction conditions, which is expected to prepare fully spectrally tunable CDs and white-light CDs. This phenomenon was reported in the study by Neeraj Soni and co-workers. [27]. They suggested that the blue fluorescence comes from the core structure of nitrogen doping and the aggregation-induced rigid structure of the molecular state [41]. However, no structures with similar lattice stripes were found for sample 220-CDs (Fig. S8, see Supporting Information). Therefore, the generation of blue fluorescence needed to further investigated. It has been reported that in the preparation of CDs using oPD. The acidic and oxidizing reaction environment was crucial for the formation of red CDs [20]. In this study, TA, as an organic acid, can provide an acidic environment as a source of carbon, but no oxidants are presented in the raw feedstock. Therefore, it is conjectured that oxygen in the vessel was involved in the formation of red-emitting CDs. To verify this hypothesis, 10 μL of 30 % hydrogen peroxide solution was added before the reaction to provide additional oxygen. The hydrogen peroxide solution was added under two preparation conditions, (i) low temperature and low concentration for a short time and (ii) high temperature and high concentration for a long time. The results are shown in Fig. 6 and Table S9 (see Supporting Information).

As shown in Fig. 6a, under mild conditions, g-CDs are the dominant luminescent material, while the r-CDs make up a small fraction. No blue luminescent peak appeared. However, upon the addition of hydrogen peroxide, the green PL peak was significantly reduced and the red emission was slightly increased. In contrast, blue ones became the dominant ones. Similar results were observed in Fig. 6b for more intense reaction conditions with enhanced blue and red PL peaks. The green emitting peaks were weak because the severe reaction conditions accelerated the PL quenching or conversion process of g-CDs, and thus the oxidation of hydrogen peroxide was not significant. It is well known that the luminescence of g-CDs mainly originated from functional groups such as surface carbonyl groups. However, the interaction of hydrogen peroxide with CO diminished fluorescence [42]. The modification of surface groups by oxidation leads to the formation of non-radiative states, or conformational breakdown of emission centers [43,44]. Moreover, increased blue and red emitting suggested that the r-CDs were from oxidation. XPS analysis also indicates sample 220-CDs with higher oxygen components than that of sample 180-CDs.

The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 6c demonstrate that the addition of H2O2 enriched the surface of CDs with additional functional groups, especially oxygen-containing functional groups. The vibrational peak of hydroxyl groups was evident at 3415 cm−1, CO increased at 1687 cm−1, CN was retained at 1635 cm−1, and the benzene ring skeleton peak is more pronounced at 1600–1450 cm−1. The peaks at 2817, 2543, and 1429 cm−1 were attributed to the N-H bonding, the CC/CN/CO was at 1275 cm−1, the C-O peak was at 1050 cm−1, the C-O-H out-of-plane bending of carboxylic acid or the symmetric stretching of carboxylic anhydride was at 951 cm−1, and the stretching vibrations of phenazine structure were at 748 and 615 cm−1 [18]. The FT-IR spectra show that the addition of hydrogen peroxide can enhance the red fluorescence intensity by enriching the CDs with more oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups, thus providing more molecular or surface-state luminescence centers.

To further explore the PL properties of CDs, the fluorescence decay plots of CDs are shown in Fig. 6d and the parameters are illustrated in Table 1. The average lifetime (τavg) of CDs was calculated by a triple exponential model using Eq. (2) below.
(2)
τ avg =
τ
1
2
B
1
+
τ
2
2
B
2
+
τ
3
2
B
3
τ
1
B
1
+
τ
2
B
2
+
τ
3
B
3
Where, τ1, τ2, and τ3 represent the decay time constant, and B1, B2, and B3 are their fractional contributions, respectively. The lifetime fitting of CDs usually presents a two-exponential decay, in which the short-lifetime component is attributed to the carbon core state and the long-lived component to the surface state or the molecular state [45,46]. The molecular state has a longer recombination lifetime than the surface state. Therefore, the longest lifetime component was presumed to be related to molecular luminescence [47,48]. In essence, the longer the lifetime component, the slower the radiation recombination process. The sp2 luminescence core of the CDs was compact, and the radiation photo was emitted quickly, resulting in short lifetime decay. On contrast, CDs with high oxidation degrees or large polarity are filled with relaxed substrates before fluorescence attenuation occurs, thus generating more ground states that contribute to fluorescence [49].

Specifically, the blue component in B-H2O2-CDs was dominated by carbon core state luminescence rather than molecular state luminescence, indicating that the blue fluorescence was not due to the molecular luminescence of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid, but due to the formation of blue carbon core during the synthesis. The solvent sensitivity of sample 180-CDs was attributed to the surface state's susceptibility towards solvent polarity and hydrogen bonding [50]. Therefore, surface state luminescence dominated in sample 180-CDs, while in r-CDs, it originated from the interaction between molecular fluorophores and carbon core state [51]. This may be because the benzene ring at the edge of the red fluorophore can bind to the carbon nucleus through a conjugated structure [33]. More importantly, it has been suggested that the longer carrier lifetime and triple exponential decay processes result from additional energy transfer from green or blue CDs to adjacent red CDs, which was also consistent with the previous speculation that FRET processes occur at red CDs [52]. To summarize, the photoluminescence of sample 180-CDs primarily originated from the surface state, whereas the r-CDs in sample 220-CDs consist of a blue sp2 luminescent core and an edge state or molecular red emission center connected to the edge of the carbon core. The FRET process occurred simultaneously in the presence of a few g-CDs [38,50].

The mechanism of CDs formation was proposed as illustrated in Fig. 6e. Firstly, 2,3- DAPN was formed by polymerization of oPD. Subsequently, under the catalysis and participation of TA, DAPN undergoes further polymerization and carbonization to form green CDs. Simultaneously, it is oxidized by oxygen in the reaction vessel to form red fluorescent molecules such as quinoxalino [2,3-b] QXPDA and DHQP on the other hand [27,33]. The temperature rise promotes the formation of red fluorophores. Moreover, large CDs were pyrolyzed into smaller ones on one hand to provide more luminescence from the carbon core. On the other hand, they may agglomerate with molecules and overlap to form larger blue CDs with low yield but high PLQYs [4]. Ultimately, due to a good overlap in the excitation-emission spectra among blue, green, and red CDs; FRET enhanced red emission.

Despite rich photochemical properties, CDs are challenging to stabilize over a long time and prone to aggregation due to high surface energy and large contact area. Therefore, it is of great significance to prepare high-performance CDs that exhibit long-time storage capability [53]. To investigate the storage stability of the CDs, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs stored at room temperature for 210 days was compared with that of the freshly prepared ones. Surprisingly, after 210 days of room temperature storage, the fluorescence intensity of 180-CDs did not decrease, and even slightly increased, possibly attributed to its self-passivation. Furthermore, the functional groups of sample 180-CDs confer them with a specific charge, leading to Coulombic repulsion among the CDs and preventing their aggregation of the CDs, thus ensuring long-term stability [54]. However, the red fluorescence of sample 220-CDs was instability with a significant decrease in intensity while green fluorescence intensity increased during storage. This suggests that the red CDs tend to form more stable structures during storage.

The CDs/polymer films, named as blue, green, and yellow, were prepared by dispersing CDs in suitable polymers. Fig. 7a and f depict the photographs of CDs/polymer films under room light and 365 nm UV light. Fig. 7d, e, g, and h show the PL spectra of 180-CDs/PS, 220-CDs/PS, 180-CDs/PVA, and 220-CDs/PVA films after continuous irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp. Compared with bare CDs, the PL of CDs/PS was blue shifted (in Fig. 7d & e),

the fluorescence of 180-CDs/PS was cyan, and the emission wavelength was 500 nm (λex=410 nm) while the fluorescence of 220-CDs/PS was green (λem=536 nm; λex=468 nm). Interestingly, the PL of 180-CDs/PS gradually red-shifted under UV light irradiation. The excitation wavelength was shifted to 468 nm and the emission wavelength was redshifted to 569 nm after 4 h, which was gradually shifted to 574 nm by further UV irradiation. This unique property indicates that 180-CDs/PS can function as sensors for light detection [55]. This phenomenon is attributed to the high optical transition states of oxygen-containing functional groups and fluorescent molecules on the surface of sample 180-CDs compared to the carbon core. UV irradiation resulted in photobleaching and deoxidation processes that alter charge carrier generation and recombination within the CDs. Consequently, the exposed carbon core serves as the primary luminous center [55,56]. After 9 h of UV irradiation, its fluorescence intensity remained at approximately 77 % of its initial intensity. The emission wavelength of 220-CDs/PS exhibited a slight change after 9 h of UV irradiation (from 536 nm to 538 nm), accompanied by decreased PL intensity to approximately 67 % of its initial value. Excessive oxidation may result in quenching of red fluorescence [57]. This indirectly confirms that luminescence from sample 220-CDs mainly arises from molecular and carbon core states while luminescence from sample 180-CDs primarily originates from surface states -- consistent with the above analysis.

The PL of CDs in PVA films also exhibited a blue shift, as depicted in Fig. 7g and Fig. 7h. The emission wavelengths of 180-CDs/PVA and 220-CDs/PVA were measured to be 543 and 568 nm (λex=468 nm), respectively. Notably, even after exposure to UV radiation for a duration of 10 h, no alteration was observed for PL peak wavelength. Moreover, the PL intensity remained at approximately 48 % and 34 %, respectively. The CDs film pictures before and after UV irradiation are shown in Fig. S9 (see Supporting Information). These findings unequivocally demonstrate the exceptional stability of CDs and their immense potential in diverse applications such as anti-counterfeiting measures and UV detection.

Green and red emitting CDs with PLQYs of 34.0 % and 26.8 %, respectively, were synthesized via a solvothermal method using oPD and TA as precursors at different temperatures. The impact of reaction parameters on the PLQYs was investigated through the response surface method, while the formation process and luminescence mechanism were comprehensively studied. Temperature and concentration emerged as crucial factors influencing the PLQYs of green CDs, whereas TA played a pivotal role in enhancing the PLQYs of red CDs. Initially, oPD underwent polymerization to yield green CDs, which subsequently oxidized with involvement of TA to generate red and blue CDs. Solvation chromism analysis, lifetime decay curve examination, and photostability testing in polymer films revealed that surface states significantly contributed to green emission while carbon core and molecular states predominantly governed red emission. Furthermore, blue, green, and yellow CD/polymer films were fabricated using 180-CDs or 220-CDs. After long-term storage, high stability was observed for 180-CDs as their fluorescence intensity remained unchanged even after being stored at room temperature for 7 months.

Yi Tian: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yunlong Yue: Writing – review & editing, Administration, Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported partly by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (grant no. 51972145), Ji Nan Science & Technology Bureau, China (No. 2021GXRC109), and Science and Technology Program of University of Jinan, China (XKY2118).
